Document [1] (Title: Baricitinib -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications) In 2020, following a clinical trial, the FDA also granted emergency use authorization (EUA) for the use of baricitinib in combination with remdesivir to treat COVID-19 in hospitalized patients who need supplemental oxygen, invasive mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). [3] Subsequently, the EUA was revised to authorize baricitinib as a standalone treatment for adult patients. However, for pediatric patients aged 2 to 17, baricitinib continues to be under EUA status. [4] Clinical trials have shown promising results for the use of baricitinib in the treatment of alopecia areata. Document [2] (Title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Treatments) Selonsertib is a first-in-class small-molecule (apoptosis signal-regulating kinase 1 [ASK1]) inhibitor developed to treat diseases with a high burden of oxidative stress, including PAH. [99] The randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2 ARROW trial included 151 study participants. [100] All were aged 18 to 75 years and had a diagnosis of PAH. From December 2014 to November 2015, patients were stratified by PAH etiology and background therapy, and underwent random assignment to selonsertib 2 mg, selonsertib 6 mg, selonsertib 18 mg, or placebo administered once daily via tablet. The primary outcome was a change in PVR from baseline to 24 weeks, as measured by RHC. Mean change in PVR was 6 dynes/cm in the placebo group, 35 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 2-mg group ( P = .21), 28 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 6-mg group ( P = .27) and –21 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 18-mg group ( P = .60). [100] Additionally, the investigators reported no improvement in secondary outcomes such as exercise capacity, symptom burden, or worsening clinical events.
Document [3] (Title: Lenvatinib -- Indications) Lenvatinib was also approved as a combination therapy with everolimus for RCC based on a randomized phase II trial. The trial compared lenvatinib 24 mg as monotherapy to everolimus 5 mg monotherapy and lenvatinib 24 mg and everolimus 5 mg as combination therapy. The combination group demonstrated a lengthened progression-free survival of 14.6 months compared to the 5.5 months of everolimus monotherapy and 7.4 months of lenvatinib monotherapy. The combination therapy also showed prolonged overall survival compared to the monotherapies. [5] Lenvatinib, in conjunction with pembrolizumab, has gained FDA approval for treating patients with advanced endometrial carcinoma proficient in mismatch repair, as diagnosed by an FDA-sanctioned test or lacking high microsatellite instability. [6] [7] [8]

Document [7] (Title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval) Eventually, Biogen completed 2 phase 3 clinical trials with identical designs. Known individually as EMERGE and ENGAGE, both trials were initially terminated on grounds of futility before they were reinstated after additional blinded data became available. Based on this additional data, the researchers determined that EMERGE had met its primary outcome measure (the CDR "sum of boxes"); ENGAGE did not meet its primary outcome, but "benefits were observed" in study participants on higher doses for more extended periods. Knopman et al analyzed the data from 2 clinical trials with aducanumab. Document [8] (Title: Adrenocortical Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapy Approach) Recently, drugs targeting IGF-II seemed to be very promising, as IGF-II is the most-up regulated gene in ACC (80% as described before). Preclinical studies antagonizing this pathway with pharmacological agents resulted in inhibition of growth in vitro and in vivo. This inhibition was more potent than that observed with the use of mitotane alone in decreasing xenograft growth, and the combination of IGF inhibition with mitotane resulted in greater antiproliferative effects than those observed with the use of single-agent treatment. These data establish the role of targeted disruption of IGF-1R signaling to obtain a therapeutic advantage when used with mitotane therapy or possibly other chemotherapeutics in ACC patients. [106] Recently, a phase 2 study used an IMCA12 (cixutumumab), a fully humanized IGF-IR antibody showed a lack of efficacy in a cohort of 19 patients. [107] Also, results of a huge phase 3 trial “GALACCTIC” with a highly specific IGF-IR inhibitor linstinib (OSI-906)in a cohort of 138 metastatic ACC have been recently published: the progression-free and overall survival did not differ between the linstinib and placebo groups. [108] However, in another study, 26 heavily pretreated ACC patients received cixutumumab with temsirolimus, an inhibitor of mammalian targets of IGF-1R signaling (mTOR inhibitor)weekly with restaging at eight weeks. Of these 26 patients, 11 (42%) had stable disease for greater than six months. [109] Finally, because of disease heterogeneity, it appears that using one single agent is not sufficient to induce an objective response. Trials with new targeted substances are underway.
Document [9] (Title: Vemurafenib -- Indications -- Indications) The study found increased overall survival and progression-free survival in the vemurafenib-treated group compared to the dacarbazine group. However, these results were partially confounded by a crossover during the interim analysis, in which patients from the dacarbazine arm were switched to vemurafenib after early data revealed greater efficacy and response rates with the BRAF inhibitor (vemurafenib). Ultimately, statistical analyses that accounted for the crossover still demonstrated a more favorable response with vemurafenib in patients with stages IIIC and IV melanoma harboring the BRAF V600E mutation. These findings led to the approval of the drug by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in August 2011. The American Society of Clinical Oncology recommends vemurafenib in combination with cobimetinib for patients with unresectable or metastatic melanoma with a BRAF V600 mutation. Alternative treatment options include combination immunotherapy with nivolumab and ipilimumab. [12]
Document [10] (Title: Adenoid Cystic Cancer -- Medical Oncology) Because ACCs do not have a fast turnover rate, systemic chemotherapy does not confer benefit generally, and therefore a standard chemotherapy regimen is not a part of treatment for ACCs. [1] However, the consensus remains to reserve chemotherapy for palliative purposes in patients with symptomatic metastatic disease, recurrent disease, or rapidly progressing tumors not amenable to surgery. Studies have also been done to look at possible molecular targets for immunotherapy agents, but results have been disappointing. Clinical trials looking at c-KIT (an onco-receptor overexpressed by ACCs) as a potential target for the c-KIT inhibitor, imatinib, showed disappointing results. [14] [15] Similarly, drugs targeting the EGFR and HER-2 receptor families did not show any clinical benefit. [16] [17] [18] However, a recent phase II trial looking at the tyrosine kinase inhibitor axitinib in patients with incurable recurrent/metastatic ACC showed that 66.7% of patients had tumor shrinkage and 9.1% of patients had a partial response suggesting that patients with 4q12 amplified ACCs may benefit from tyrosine kinase inhibitors. [19] NTRK inhibitors such as larotrectinib should be considered in NTRK mutations in the absence of other available options in recurrent or metastatic tumors, as approved by the FDA.

Document [17] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors Baricitinib is an oral selective inhibitor of Janus kinase (JAK) 1 and JAK 2 currently indicated for moderate to severely active rheumatoid arthritis patients. Baricitinib was considered a potential treatment for COVID-19 based on its inhibitory effect on SARS-CoV-2 endocytosis in vitro and on the intracellular signaling pathway of cytokines that cause the late-onset hyperinflammatory state that results in severe illness. [115] [116] This dual inhibitory effect makes it a promising therapeutic drug against all stages of COVID-19. A multicenter observational, retrospective study of 113 hospitalized patients with COVID-19 pneumonia who received baricitinib combined with lopinavir/ritonavir (baricitinib arm, n=113) or hydroxychloroquine and lopinavir/ritonavir (control arm, n=78) reported significant improvement in clinical symptoms and 2-week mortality rate in the baricitinib arm compared with the control arm. Results from the ACTT-2 trial, a double-blind, randomized placebo-controlled trial evaluating baricitinib plus remdesivir in hospitalized adult patients with COVID-19, reported that the combination therapy of baricitinib plus remdesivir was superior to remdesivir therapy alone in not only reducing recovery time but also accelerating clinical improvement in hospitalized patients with COVID-19, particularly who were receiving high flow oxygen supplementation or non-invasive ventilation. [117] Baricitinib, in combination with remdesivir, has been approved for clinical use in hospitalized patients with COVID-19 under a EUA issued by the FDA. The efficacy of baricitinib alone or in combination with remdesivir has not been evaluated in the SARS-CoV-2 variants, and there is limited data on the use of baricitinib with dexamethasone. Ruxolitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and 2 indicated for myeloproliferative disorders, polycythemia vera, and steroid-resistant GVHD. Similar to baricitinib, it has been hypothesized to have an inhibitory effect on cytokines' intracellular signaling pathway, making it a potential treatment against COVID-19. Results from a small prospective multicenter randomized controlled phase 2 trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of ruxolitinib reported no statistical difference from the standard of care. However, most patients demonstrated significant chest CT improvement and faster recovery from lymphopenia. [118] A large randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled multicenter trial (NCT04362137) is ongoing to assess ruxolitinib's efficacy and safety in patients with severe COVID-19. Tofacitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and JAK3 that is indicated for moderate to severe RA, psoriatic arthritis, and moderate to severe ulcerative colitis. Given its inhibitory effect on the inflammatory cascade, it was hypothesized that its use could ameliorate the viral inflammation-mediated lung injury in patients with severe COVID-19. Results from a small randomized controlled trial that evaluated the efficacy involving 289 patients who were randomized to receive tofacitinib or placebo showed that tofacitinib led to a lower risk of respiratory failure or death (PMID:34133856).
Document [18] (Title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations) Breastfeeding considerations: During trials, baricitinib was detected in rat milk. Although it is unknown whether baricitinib is present in human milk, breastfeeding mothers are advised against its use. Preferably, an alternative drug should be considered, especially when nursing a newborn or preterm infant. The manufacturer recommends refraining from nursing during therapy and for 4 days after the last dose. [16] Pediatric patients: The FDA has issued the EUA for baricitinib to treat COVID-19 in hospitalized pediatric patients aged 2 to 17 who require supplemental oxygen, ECMO, or mechanical ventilation. [17] Older patients: No specific dose adjustment for baricitinib has been recommended for the older population.

Document [25] (Title: Bronchial Carcinoid Tumors (Archived) -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials) Very few clinical trials have been performed in regard to the medical management of pulmonary, endocrine tumors because of its rarity.  However, there are ongoing randomized control trials such as Alliance A021602: CABINET. This trial is currently comparing the effects of cabozantinib vs. placebo for those patients with well-differentiated bronchial carcinoids. Other ongoing trials include an open-label phase II trial that is currently assessing the use of ipilimumab and nivolumab in advanced disease. The data from these studies are still being collected with anticipated results available in the near future. Ongoing studies such as these may highlight a much larger role of medical management as compared to surgical resection. [9]
Document [26] (Title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Introduction -- The need for additional therapeutic agents) Over the course of the past year, our understanding of the pathophysiology of COVID-19 has improved. With ongoing randomized controlled trials evaluating therapies for COVID -19, like the RECOVERY Trial recruiting over 39,000 individuals, the guidelines for therapy for COVID-19 remain fluid. Despite a better understanding of the disease, the mortality and morbidity associated with COVID-19 remains unacceptably high.  The recent wave of overwhelming cases in the Republic of India is a grave example of the potential for disaster with COVID-19. There is a need for agents that can be added to the existing arsenal of approved medications. An ideal therapeutic agent should be reliable with robust clinical benefits. The recommendation for its use should be backed by evidence, and it should have a favorable and benign side effect profile. Apart from this, the agent should be readily available to the masses, should be inexpensive, and should be able to meet an exorbitant demand with an efficient supply chain. In an attempt to apply these criteria through this review; we evaluate the evidence for and against the concurrent use of Remdesivir and Baricitinib, indications and contraindications, mechanism of action, and the side effect profile of these drugs.
Document [27] (Title: Squamous Cell Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Targeted Therapy) EGFR exon 19 deletion or exon 21 L858R mutations : Osimertinib is the preferred agent. [9] At progression there, patients can be treated with doublet chemotherapy and immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. Patients treated with either erlotinib, afatinib, gefitinib, or dacomitinib in the first-line setting can be switched to osimertinib at progression after confirming T790M mutation either on liquid biopsy or tumor sample. [10] EGFR S768I, L861Q, or G719X mutations : Preferred agents for these groups of patients include afatinib or osimertinib. KRAS G12C : These patients are initially treated based on PD-L1 status and, at progression, are treated with either sotorasib or adagrasib. [11] [12] ALK rearrangement : When ALK rearrangement is detected before the initiation of any systemic therapy for advanced SCC of the lung, it is preferred to use TKI therapy using agents alectinib, brigatinib, or lorlatinib. [13] [14] [15]
Document [28] (Title: Lenvatinib -- Indications) Lenvatinib was approved for use in radioactive iodine-refractory DTC following a randomized, multicenter, placebo-controlled phase III study (SELECT). The trial included 392 participants with radioactive iodine-refractory DTC and was subject to random assignment to either a treatment group of 24 mg lenvatinib daily or the placebo group at a 2:1 ratio. The treatment group demonstrated a median progression-free survival of 18.3 months and a response rate of 64.8% compared to the placebo group, 3.6 months and 1.5%, respectively. Both groups' overall survival did not show a notable difference, which may have been from the crossover between the placebo and treatment groups. [1] Lenvatinib has also been studied for use in anaplastic thyroid cancer but is currently not FDA-approved for this indication. [2]
Document [29] (Title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Poly(ADP)-ribose polymerase inhibitors) PARP inhibitors have recently gained momentum for the maintenance treatment of ovarian cancer. PARP inhibitor agents include olaparib, niraparib, and rucaparib. Olaparib was the first Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved drug in this subgroup indicated to treat advanced BRCA mutated ovarian cancer after platinum-based chemotherapy, based on SOLO-1, phase 3 randomized double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Olaparib reduced disease progression or death by 70% (hazard ratio 0.30, 0.23 to 0.41; P < .001). [51] The PAOLA-1 trial, a phase 3 randomized controlled trial of 806 women with stage III to IV high-grade serous or endometrioid ovarian cancer, showed a progression-free survival benefit of 4.5 months in the group that received olaparib and bevacizumab maintenance versus placebo and bevacizumab. [52] This combination of olaparib, niraparib, or rucaparib with bevacizumab achieved FDA and NCCN approval as a first-line maintenance treatment for these patients with ovarian cancer after initial platinum-based chemotherapy with partial or complete response or tumors associated with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) defined by the presence of deleterious BRCA mutation. Recent clinical trials include the VELIA and PRIMA trials using a newer PARP inhibitor, veliparib, and niraparib maintenance therapy, respectively, showed markedly improved progression-free survival compared to the placebo group in patients with newly diagnosed advanced-stage ovarian cancer who initially responded to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. [53] [54]

The update reaffirms the 2018 "HER2 Breast Testing Guideline Focused Update" and was prompted by the results of the 2022 DESTINY-Breast 04 trial, which led to the expansion of the approval of the HER2 antibody-drug conjugate, trastuzumab deruxtecan, by the United States Food and Drug Administration (US FDA) to include metastatic patients with HER2 immunohistochemistry (IHC) 1+ or 2+/in situ hybridization (ISH) negative results. However, the guideline update does not support the use of a HER2-Low interpretive category due to the lack of evidence to support that IHC 1+ or 2+/ISH negative results are predictive of trastuzumab deruxtecan treatment response when compared to IHC 0 results. Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.
Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.
Document [2] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.
Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.
Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327) Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.
Document [5] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 88) BCR‑ABL inhibitors—including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib—are classified as protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; dasatinib, its anhydrous form and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.
Document [6] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 37) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib; they inhibit the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase pathway and are employed in hematologic malignancies, with dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib and imatinib mesylát indicated for lymphoid leukemia and ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, another protein‑kinase inhibitor subgroup, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib and target Bruton's tyrosine‑kinase; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, highlighting BTK inhibition in lymphoid cancers. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class and exemplify kinase‑targeted therapy beyond leukemias. The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors” also contains agents such as cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin have been identified as potential treatments for acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these protein‑kinase inhibitors—targeting BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases—constitute a network of antineoplastic and immunomodulating drugs that underpin contemporary therapeutic strategies for lymphoid and myeloid leukemias and other cancers.

Document [8] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. Document [9] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326) Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.
Document [10] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81) Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916) Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors.
